frameworks.name: Emmert's law (size-distance scaling)
frameworks.one_line: Perceived afterimage size scales with perceived distance of the projection plane.

frameworks.name: Ponzo illusion (linear perspective)
frameworks.one_line: Pictorial depth cues induce illusory distance that biases perceived size.

frameworks.name: Top-down feedback from higher-level visual cortex
frameworks.one_line: Depth markers persist via feedback even when retinal depth cues vanish.

core_question: Does illusory distance from pictorial depth cues modulate afterimage size even after the depth cues disappear?
key_assumptions: Afterimages reflect retinal-level adaptation but are subject to perceived-distance scaling
Linear perspective converging lines induce reliable illusory depth
A depth marker can be bound to an object across cue offsets
manipulations: Static vs flickering perspective cues during adaptation/test
Position of horizontal bar (upper farther vs lower nearer in Ponzo layout)
Presence vs absence of depth cues at the moment of size judgment
key_findings: Static perspective cues produced standard Ponzo size-distance illusion on afterimages
Flickering perspective cues left only the bar afterimage visible, yet substantial illusory size bias persisted
Illusory size modulation outlasted the retinal availability of the depth cues
interpretations: Co-occurrence of depth cues and object binds a depth marker to the object
Perceived size is modulated by feedback of depth information from higher-level visual cortex
